content.text:
A **tree** combines connectivity with the absence of 
cycles—a minimal structure for linking all vertices.

content.title: Tree
content.content: A **tree** is a connected graph with no cycles (simple cycles).

content.title: Forest
content.content:
A **forest** is a graph with no cycles (not necessarily 
connected). Each connected component of a forest is a tree.

content.title: Characterizations of Trees
content.content:
For a graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, the following are equivalent:

1. $G$ is a tree (connected and acyclic).

2. $G$ is connected and has exactly $n - 1$ edges.

3. $G$ is acyclic and has exactly $n - 1$ edges.

4. There is a unique path between any two vertices.

5. $G$ is connected, but removing any edge disconnects it.

6. $G$ is acyclic, but adding any edge creates exactly one cycle.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(1) $\Rightarrow$ (2): Induction on $n$. Base: one vertex, zero edges. 
Inductive: remove a leaf (degree-1 vertex, which must exist in a tree); 
remaining graph is a tree with $n-1$ vertices and $n-2$ edges by IH.

(2) $\Rightarrow$ (3): If connected with $n-1$ edges had a cycle, removing 
a cycle edge keeps it connected with fewer edges, contradicting minimality.

Other implications follow similarly.

content.title: Trees and non-trees
content.problem:
- $K_1$, $K_2$, $P_n$: trees

- $K_3$, $C_n$, $K_4$: not trees (contain cycles)

- Star graph $K_{1,n}$: tree (one center connected to $n$ leaves)

content.title: Leaf
content.content: A **leaf** (or pendant vertex) is a vertex of degree 1.

content.title: Leaves in Trees
content.content: Every tree with $\geq 2$ vertices has at least two leaves.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $P = v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_k$ be a longest path in tree $T$. Then 
$v_0$ and $v_k$ are leaves: if $v_0$ had neighbor $u \neq v_1$, either 
$u$ is on $P$ (creating a cycle) or $u, v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_k$ is a 
longer path. Both are contradictions. Similarly for $v_k$.

marginNotes.text: Every good tree bears good fruit.
